title_en: Structure and Methodology: The Science of Legal Distinctions
content_en:
Al-Furuq, whose full title is Anwar al-Buruq fi Anwa al-Furuq (The Lights of Dawns in the Categories of Legal Distinctions), consists of 548 legal distinctions (furuq) in four volumes. The work's organizing principle is deceptively simple: in each entry, al-Qarafi identifies two or more cases that appear superficially similar and explains why they receive different legal rulings. The intellectual value of this exercise is significant: by articulating the distinction that justifies different treatment, al-Qarafi forces explicit recognition of the underlying principles of Maliki jurisprudence — principles that were previously applied by trained jurists but rarely stated with precision.

The methodology of Al-Furuq is closely connected to al-Qarafi's broader theory of legal principles. He distinguished between two types of universal propositions in Islamic law: al-qawa'id al-kulliyya (general legal rules) and al-dawabit (specific regulatory principles within individual domains). The legal distinctions he draws in Al-Furuq often turn on the difference between cases governed by different qawa'id — a distinction that would be invisible to anyone who had not mastered the underlying principle structure. In this way, Al-Furuq and his works on legal maxims are deeply interconnected.

A characteristic feature of al-Qarafi's methodology is his willingness to identify cases where the Maliki school's practice deviates from what its own stated principles would require. Rather than defending every received position, he critically examines the school's doctrine from the standpoint of its internal logic. This self-critical approach gives the work an intellectual integrity that purely defensive or apologetic treatments of legal doctrine lack.

The work is also notable for al-Qarafi's engagement with scholars from the other schools, particularly the Shafi'i tradition. Many of the distinctions he draws are developed in dialogue with Shafi'i positions, and al-Qarafi is generous in acknowledging where the Shafi'i analysis illuminates a problem. This cross-school dialogue reflects his broader conviction that the various Sunni legal traditions are complementary rather than simply competitive: each has captured aspects of the truth that the others can benefit from understanding. The scale of the work — 548 distinctions — gives it an encyclopedic character while the consistent analytical format maintains intellectual coherence throughout.
